FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; baseline 9-11 years from 21-site NIH-funded study; methods specify 21-site adolescent brain cognitive development study

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; generalized linear mixed models analyzing relationship between electronic media use and anxiety/depression

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Child Psychiatry & Human Development (2019)

FT_IC2.evidence: Electronic media use variables (television, videos, video games, video chatting, texting, social networking sites) paired with individual-trait outcomes (anxiety and depression); both child and parent reports of EMU examined

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative results reported; regression coefficients for anxiety and depression relationships (e.g., child weekday EMU predicting anxiety t=3.309, p=0.001)

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple regression coefficients with p-values and 95% confidence intervals provided for EMU-anxiety and EMU-depression associations

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; FT-EC7 applies only to longitudinal designs
notes: Study controlled for age, sex, BMI, family income, race, and physical activity; examined both weekday and weekend EMU across multiple media types

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; baseline data 2016-18; 11,875 children aged 9-10 years; broadly US representative sample

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; negative binomial structural equation modeling with mediation analysis testing relationships between screen time types/content and problem behaviors

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity (2019)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time types (television/movies, videos, video games, social media) and content (mature-rated video games, R-rated movies) paired with individual-trait outcomes (problem behaviors including aggressive behavior, rule-breaking, social problems, thought problems, etc.)

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative results reported; incidence rate ratios (IRR) for direct and indirect effects (e.g., television/movies IRR=1.059 for rule-breaking, p<0.001)

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple IRR values with 95% confidence intervals reported across all ST types and problem behavior outcomes; mediation indirect effects also quantified

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional baseline data; FT-EC7 applies only to longitudinal designs
notes: Large representative sample; controlled for sex, ethnicity, parental education, family income, body mass index, and physical activity; examined both ST types and content

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; first annual curated release; participants aged 8-11 years; 4524 children from 21 ABCD sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; structural equation modeling examining associations between meeting Canadian 24-Hour Movement Guidelines (including screen time ≤2 hours/day) and impulsivity dimensions

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time (recreational ST ≤2 hours/day recommendation, measured via Youth Screen Time Survey) paired with individual-trait outcomes (impulsivity dimensions: negative urgency, positive urgency, perseverance, BIS, BAS reward responsiveness, BAS drive, BAS fun-seeking, delay-discounting)

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative results reported; standardized path coefficients (β) for movement behavior combinations with impulsivity outcomes (e.g., ST+sleep recommendations β=−.12 for negative urgency, p<0.001)

FT_IC4.evidence: Standardized β coefficients with standard errors and 95% confidence intervals provided across all impulsivity dimensions; structural model estimates reported with goodness-of-fit indices

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; FT-EC7 applies only to longitudinal designs
notes: Examined concurrent associations between meeting movement behavior recommendations and impulsivity dimensions; controlled for sex, BMI, family income, parental education, and ethnicity
